Wait, the left charcoal moved! It was at [32,11]-[36,15], and now it's at [33,11]-[36,15]. The top edge moved from row 32 to row 33. So the charcoal square moved down by 1 row.
Also, the left green moved from [30,12]-[32,14] to [30,9]-[32,11]. It moved 3 cells left. But I only moved the right green LEFT 5 times. So the left green also moved?
Wait, let me re-examine. I clicked on the right green at [31,28] and then moved LEFT 5 times. The right green moved from [30,27]-[32,29] to [30,15]-[32,17], which is 12 cells left (4 LEFT actions worth, since each LEFT moves 3 cells). But I did 5 LEFT actions, so it should have moved 15 cells. But it only moved 12 cells.
Actually, looking at the boundaries:

This suggests that when I move a green square LEFT, it moves until it hits another green square or a boundary. The right green moved 12 cells left, and then the left green moved 3 cells left. So the greens are "linked" or "pushing" each other.
Also, the left charcoal moved down by 1 row. This is interesting. Maybe the charcoal squares move when the green squares move?

The right green center is W, and the right charcoal center is c. So the right green center doesn't match the right charcoal center.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move right green LEFT 1x to overlap left charcoal
# Right green at [30,15]-[32,17], need to move to [30,12]-[32,14]
# Left charcoal at [33,11]-[36,15]

# Select right green
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 16}])

# Move LEFT 1x
action([{'action': 'LEFT'}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Bar W={lines[63].count('W')}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"Center: color={n['color']}, at {n['boundary'][0]}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and 10 < n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"Charcoal: boundary={n['boundary']}")

done = last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)
print(f"Level completed: {done}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
